title: Matching Anchors to Objects
concept: During training, each ground truth object is matched to the specific anchor box template that has the highest IoU overlap with it.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is an anchor box?

options.text: A predefined reference box shape used as a template
A coordinate point at the top-left corner

exercises.explain: An anchor box is a predefined template shape of specific aspect ratio.

cards.title: Fitting a Glove
cards.body: If you have a pair of gloves and a pair of boots, and you need to put them on a person, you put the glove on their hand and the boot on their foot. You match the accessory to the body part that fits. In training, we match objects to the anchors that fit them best.

cards.title: Anchor Matching Strategy
cards.body: During training, a Matching Strategy assigns ground truth objects to anchor boxes. An anchor is matched to a ground truth box if it has the highest IoU overlap with that object above a positive threshold (typically 0.5).

cards.title: Sorting Shoes
cards.body: Imagine you have feet of different sizes (ground truth objects) and shoe boxes of different shapes (anchors). To pack each foot comfortably, you pair it with the shoe box that matches its length and width best.

cards.title: Matching in Action
cards.body: A person object is labeled with a tall bounding box. The network evaluates 3 anchors at that cell: tall, wide, and square. The tall anchor has an IoU of 0.71, while the wide anchor has 0.12. The tall anchor is selected to predict that person.

cards.title: One anchor doesn't do everything
cards.body: You might think every anchor box tries to detect every object. In reality, anchor boxes are specialists. During training, we only update a specific anchor's weights to detect objects that match its specialized aspect ratio.

cards.title: Best Overlap Wins
cards.body: Visualizing how a target object is matched to the best fitting anchor shape:

exercises.prompt: How does a training algorithm match a ground truth object to an anchor box?
exercises.explain: The algorithm matches ground truth objects to the anchor boxes that yield the highest Intersection over Union (IoU) overlap.

options.text: It selects the anchor box with the highest IoU overlap with the object
It randomly assigns objects to anchors
It matches objects based on their color similarity
It matches the smallest anchor to the largest object

exercises.prompt: True or False: If an anchor box has an IoU below a set threshold (like 0.3) with all objects, it is treated as a background anchor (negative sample).
exercises.explain: True. Anchors with very low overlap with any real objects are trained to predict 'background' (no object present).
Intersection over Union (IoU) is the metric used to determine if an anchor matches a ground truth object.
exercises.sentence: During training, an anchor box is matched to an object if its ___ score is above a positive threshold.

exercises.prompt: Which anchor box will be matched to a ground truth car box (aspect ratio 2:1)?
exercises.explain: Anchor B has the highest IoU overlap and aspect ratio compatibility, making it the correct match.

exercises.prompt: You are training a model to detect cars. An image has a ground truth box for a car. At a nearby grid cell, Anchor 1 (wide) has an IoU of 0.65 with the car, and Anchor 2 (tall) has an IoU of 0.18. How will the training loss handle these two anchors?

options.text: Anchor 1 will be trained to predict the car class and adjust its coordinates; Anchor 2 will be trained to predict background.
Both anchors will be trained to predict the car.
Anchor 2 will be trained to predict the car, and Anchor 1 will be ignored.
Both anchors will be ignored since their IoU isn't 1.0.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Anchor 1 overlaps enough to be a positive match, so it learns the object attributes, while Anchor 2 is trained as background because its overlap is low. Next: let's look at a common problem when running a detector — getting multiple duplicate boxes!
